front: What is Performance in IT context?
back: Performance refers to how well a computer system or network operates in terms of responsiveness, throughput, and resource utilization. It measures the efficiency of system operations.
Throughput is the amount of work or data processed by the system in a given time period. Measured in transactions per second (TPS), requests per second, or operations per second.
Response time is the elapsed time from when a user initiates a request to when the system provides the complete response. It includes processing time, queue time, and transmission time.

CPU utilization measures the percentage of time the CPU is actively processing tasks. High utilization (>80%) may indicate performance bottlenecks, while very low utilization suggests underutilization.
Latency is the delay between a request and the corresponding response. It includes network latency (transmission delay), storage latency (disk access time), and processing latency.
Capacity planning is the process of determining the future IT infrastructure requirements to meet anticipated workload demands. It ensures adequate resources without over-provisioning.

front: What is Bottleneck in system performance?
back: A bottleneck is a component or resource that limits overall system performance. When a component operates at full capacity, it restricts the throughput of the entire system.
Benchmarking is the process of comparing system performance against standard reference systems or industry benchmarks to evaluate relative performance.
Load testing evaluates system behavior under expected load conditions by simulating multiple concurrent users or transactions to measure performance metrics.
Scalability is the ability of a system to handle increased workload by adding resources (vertical) or nodes (horizontal) without significant performance degradation.
Performance monitoring is the continuous collection and analysis of system metrics to identify performance issues, trends, and capacity constraints in real-time.
SLA is a contract that defines expected service performance metrics such as response time, availability, and throughput that the service provider must meet.
Disk I/O wait is the percentage of time the CPU is idle while waiting for disk operations to complete. High I/O wait indicates storage performance issues.
Peak load is the maximum workload the system experiences during its busiest period. Capacity planning must account for peak load to ensure acceptable performance.
Performance tuning is the process of optimizing system configuration and resource allocation to improve performance metrics like response time and throughput.
